notes.anchor: at Formiae, when you set off to join Pompey
notes.note: Cicero's villa at Formiae on the Latian coast was his base of indecision in 49 BC; from there he finally sailed in June to join Pompey's camp in Greece, the choice this whole letter laments.

notes.anchor: C. Curio --- the man whose friendship dragged me, step by step, into this ruined business
notes.note: C. Scribonius Curio, tribune of 50 BC, who was bought over to Caesar's side and carried Caelius with him; he had since died fighting for Caesar in Africa (Aug. 49), which sharpens Caelius's regret.

notes.anchor: charging me with errands of peace to Caesar and playing the marvellous citizen
notes.note: In the abortive negotiations of early 49 BC Cicero acted as a would-be peacemaker, carrying messages between the camps; Caelius mocks the pose ('mirificum civem agis') as having cost him personally.

notes.anchor: apart from a handful of money-lenders
notes.note: The faeneratores (creditors and financiers) backed Caesar because they feared Pompey's victory would bring debt-cancellation and proscription; Caelius says they are the only non-Pompeians left in the district.

notes.anchor: not one order that is not Pompeian
notes.note: The ordines were Rome's formal social ranks (senatorial, equestrian, and the local town councils); Caelius reports that every class on the spot, money-lenders aside, favours Pompey.

notes.anchor: call me a second Cato of Arruntanus
notes.note: A textually corrupt phrase ('Arruntanum me Catonem'); the sense is a sardonic self-comparison to Cato the moral hardliner, but both the wording and the reference are uncertain and editors despair of it.
